---
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../course-outline.css
    slide-number: true
    center: false
    margin: 0.05
    scrollable: true
    footer: "<a href='javascript:history.back()'>← Back</a>"
---

```{=html}
<section class="slide level2">
  <h2>I. Introduction</h2>

  <details>
    <summary><strong>A. Overview — Watch Video</strong></summary>
    <details>
      <summary class="lv-1">1. Bible Project — The Book of John</summary>
      <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://bibleproject.com/guides/book-of-john/" target="_blank">bibleproject.com/guides/book-of-john 1-12/</a></div>
      <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://bibleproject.com/videos/john-13-21/" target="_blank">bibleproject.com/guides/book-of-john 13-21/</a></div>
    </details>
  </details>

  <details>
    <summary><strong>B. The Book</strong></summary>
    <details>
      <summary class="lv-1">1. Authorship and Date</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <strong>Church Fathers' Views</strong> (2nd–4th centuries AD) — unanimous attribution to John the Apostle</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Written in Ephesus late in John's life, after the Synoptic Gospels had circulated</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Often called a &quot;spiritual Gospel&quot; emphasizing deeper theological truths</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Irenaeus (c. 180 AD, disciple of Polycarp who knew the apostles): &quot;John, the disciple of the Lord… did himself publish a Gospel during his residence at Ephesus in Asia&quot;</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Clement of Alexandria (c. 215 AD): urged by friends and inspired by the Spirit to write a &quot;spiritual Gospel&quot; after his oral ministry in Asia</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Origen (c. 185–254 AD) and Eusebius (c. 260–340 AD): both affirmed apostolic authorship; listed as undisputed in the church canon</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Also attested by Tertullian, the Muratorian Canon, and Justin Martyr</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Small dissenting group (the Alogi) rejected it for theological reasons — not the mainstream view</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <strong>Modern Scholarly Views on Authorship</strong></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— The Gospel is formally anonymous — never names its author</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Refers to the &quot;disciple whom Jesus loved&quot; as source of testimony (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+21%3A20-24&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 21:20–24</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+13%3A23&version=WEB" target="_blank">13:23</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Majority consensus: not directly authored by John the Apostle; product of a Johannine community or &quot;school&quot;</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Reasons: sophisticated Greek style, strong Judean focus, distinct theology vs. Synoptics</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— The Beloved Disciple was a real historical figure; exact identity unknown (candidates: John, Lazarus, a Jerusalem priest)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Minority/traditionalist view: Leon Morris, John A.T. Robinson, Richard Bauckham support direct or close connection to John the Apostle</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <strong>Modern Scholarly Views on Date</strong></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Broad consensus: c. 90–100 AD — latest of the four canonical Gospels, likely composed in Ephesus</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Earliest manuscript (P52) dated c. 125–150 AD sets the upper limit</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Gnostic writers like Heracleon commented on it by the mid-2nd century</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Minority: some conservatives (Robinson, Wright, Bauckham) argue pre-70 AD based on detailed Jerusalem topography and no explicit reference to Temple's fall</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">—Very late 2nd-century dates largely faded after manuscript evidence (e.g., P52 ~125–150 CE) showed the text already existed early in the 2nd century. Today’s critical consensus (including Ehrman) favors late 1st century (~90–100 CE), not mid-2nd. </div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. <strong>Passage missing from early manuscripts</strong></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+7%3A53-8%3A11&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 7:53–8:11</a> (the story of the woman caught in adultery, or Pericope Adulterae) Not present in any Greek manuscripts before the 5th century; style and vocabulary differ from the rest of John. Modern translations usually bracket it or footnote: “The earliest manuscripts do not include 7:53–8:11.”</div>
    </details>
    <details>
      <summary class="lv-1-gap">2. Stated Purpose</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc-italic">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+20%3A31&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 20:31</a> — &quot;…but these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name.&quot;</div>
    </details>
    <details>
      <summary class="lv-1-gap">3. Distinctives of John</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. ~80–90% of content is unique — not found in Matthew, Mark, or Luke</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. Seven &quot;I AM&quot; Statements</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">1) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+6%3A35&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 6:35</a> — &quot;I am the bread of life&quot;</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">2) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+8%3A12&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 8:12</a> — &quot;I am the light of the world&quot;</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">3) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+10%3A7&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 10:7,9</a> — &quot;I am the door/gate&quot;</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">4) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+10%3A11&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 10:11,14</a> — &quot;I am the good shepherd&quot;</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">5) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+11%3A25&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 11:25</a> — &quot;I am the resurrection and the life&quot;</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">6) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+14%3A6&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 14:6</a> — &quot;I am the way, the truth, and the life&quot;</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">7) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+15%3A1&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 15:1,5</a> — &quot;I am the true vine&quot;</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. Seven Signs</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">1) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+2%3A1-11&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 2:1–11</a> — Water into wine (Cana)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">2) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+4%3A43-54&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 4:43–54</a> — Healing of the official's son</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">3) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+5%3A1-15&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 5:1–15</a> — Healing at the pool of Bethesda</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">4) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+6%3A1-14&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 6:1–14</a> — Feeding of the five thousand</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">5) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+6%3A16-21&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 6:16–21</a> — Jesus walking on water</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">6) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+9%3A1-41&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 9:1–41</a> — Healing of the man born blind</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">7) <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+11%3A1-44&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 11:1–44</a> — Raising of Lazarus</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. Seven Titles of Jesus in John 1</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">1) Lamb of God — <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+1%3A29&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 1:29</a> (declared by John the Baptist)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">2) Rabbi / Teacher — <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+1%3A38&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 1:38</a> (used by the first disciples)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">3) Messiah / Christ — <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+1%3A41&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 1:41</a> (proclaimed by Andrew)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">4) Jesus of Nazareth — <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+1%3A45&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 1:45</a> (identified by Philip)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">5) Son of God — <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+1%3A49&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 1:49</a> (confessed by Nathanael)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">6) King of Israel — <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+1%3A49&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 1:49</a> (also confessed by Nathanael)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">7) Son of Man — <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+1%3A51&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 1:51</a> (Jesus' own self-designation)</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">e. Teaching on Holy spirit John 14,16</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">1) Holy spirit teaching — <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John%2014%3A14-16%3A15&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 14-16</a> (covered in future lesson)</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">f. High Preistly Prayer John 17</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">1) Preistly Prayer — <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John%2017&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 17</a> (covered in future lesson)</div>
    </details>
  </details>
  <details>
    <summary><strong>C. About the Author</strong></summary>
    <details>
      <summary class="lv-1">1. Life of John</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. Born c. AD 6–15, Galilee (likely Bethsaida or Capernaum); son of Zebedee and Salome; brother of James the apostle</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. Called among the first disciples; inner circle with Peter and James</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. The &quot;Beloved Disciple&quot; — remained at the crucifixion; entrusted with Mary's care</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. Pillar of the Jerusalem church (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Gal+2%3A9&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 2:9</a>); founded churches in Asia Minor</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">e. Exiled to Patmos under Emperor Domitian (c. AD 95) — wrote Revelation there</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">f. Released under Nerva (AD 96–98); returned to Ephesus</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">g. Died naturally in Ephesus c. AD 100 — only apostle not martyred</div>
    </details>
    <details>
      <summary class="lv-1-gap">2. Christian Tradition</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. Attributed authorship: Gospel of John, 1–3 John, Revelation</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. Known for teaching: <em>&quot;Little children, love one another&quot;</em> (Jerome)</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. Plunged into boiling oil in Rome under Domitian — emerged unharmed; then exiled to Patmos</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Earliest written reference: Tertullian (c. 200 AD), who mentions it as a well-known miracle</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. Fled Ephesian bathhouse on seeing the heretic Cerinthus</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">e. Disciple chain: John → Polycarp → Irenaeus — key line of apostolic tradition</div>
    </details>
    <details>
      <summary class="lv-1-gap">3. John in the Gospels and Acts</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. Early Ministry</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Called by the Sea of Galilee with brother James (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matt+4%3A21&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matt 4:21–22</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+1%3A19&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 1:19–20</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+5%3A10&version=WEB" target="_blank">Luke 5:10</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Named &quot;Sons of Thunder&quot; (Boanerges) by Jesus (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+3%3A17&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 3:17</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Inner circle witness to raising of Jairus' daughter (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+5%3A37&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 5:37</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+8%3A51&version=WEB" target="_blank">Luke 8:51</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Sent out with the Twelve (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matt+10%3A1&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matt 10</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+6%3A7&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 6:7</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+9%3A1&version=WEB" target="_blank">Luke 9:1</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Asked to call down fire on a Samaritan village; Jesus rebukes him (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+9%3A51&version=WEB" target="_blank">Luke 9:51–56</a>)</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. Mid-Ministry</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Witnesses the Transfiguration with Peter and James (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matt+17%3A1&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matt 17:1–9</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+9%3A2&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 9:2–10</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+9%3A28&version=WEB" target="_blank">Luke 9:28–36</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Reports stopping an unauthorized exorcist; Jesus corrects him (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+9%3A49&version=WEB" target="_blank">Luke 9:49–50</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Requests seats of honor in Christ's kingdom (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matt+20%3A20&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matt 20:20–28</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+10%3A35&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 10:35–45</a>)</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. Passion Week</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Sent with Peter to prepare the Passover meal (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+22%3A8&version=WEB" target="_blank">Luke 22:8</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— The &quot;Beloved Disciple&quot; reclining next to Jesus at the Last Supper (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+13%3A23&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 13:23–25</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Among the three in Gethsemane during Jesus' agony (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matt+26%3A36&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matt 26:36–37</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+14%3A32&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 14:32–33</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Gains access to the high priest's courtyard during Jesus' trial (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+18%3A15&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 18:15–16</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Present at the cross; Jesus entrusts Mary to his care (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+19%3A25&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 19:25–27</a>)</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. Resurrection Events</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Runs with Peter to the empty tomb and believes (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+20%3A2&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 20:2–8</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— First to recognize the risen Jesus by the Sea of Galilee (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+21%3A7&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 21:7</a>)</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">e. In the Book of Acts</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Listed among apostles waiting in the Upper Room (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+1%3A13&version=WEB" target="_blank">Acts 1:13</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Accompanies Peter in healing the lame man at the Temple (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+3%3A1&version=WEB" target="_blank">Acts 3:1–11</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Stands boldly with Peter before the Sanhedrin (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+4%3A1&version=WEB" target="_blank">Acts 4:1–22</a>)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Lays hands on new Samaritan believers with Peter (<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+8%3A14&version=WEB" target="_blank">Acts 8:14–17</a>)</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">f. Summary</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Consistently part of the inner circle (Peter, James, John)</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Present at key revelation moments: Transfiguration and Resurrection</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Witness to the full Passion narrative</div>
      <div class="lv-detail">— Early leader in the Jerusalem church</div>
    </details>
  </details>

</section>
```
